item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The inclusion of Oneroi as a "this is slightly more serious than initially thought" element was well-done. I get that it's the Gulf's hat, but I enjoy articles that focus on local anomalies that are in some way a part of the social fabric of small to midsized areas.

item.title: Concurrent Ideas
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I cant believe that nobody else has pointed this out, but on October 15th, 2014, Welcome to Night Vale (a podcast where pretty much every other sentence has (by foundation standards) an anomaly or cognitohazard) had an episode about a homecoming game that had to be cancelled because one of the players, Maliq Herrera, was found to not actually exist (https://nightvale.fandom.com/wiki/Maliq_Herrera). I love cross-fandom things like this, even though they aren't truly related.

"This just in: There’s news breaking of a possible scandal involving one of the Night Vale football players. Senior running back Maliq Herrera, according to an investigative report by Leann Hart of the Night Vale Daily Journal, Herrera doesn’t actually exist. Like, he’s right there when you look at him, and he plays in a team of other players, so it would make more sense for him to be there than not. But, Hart claims in her report that the all-district running back is, in fact, completely fabricated.

Anonymous sources say that Herrera is collectively imagined by the Night Vale High School fan base – a shared dream…"

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image contained copyrighted (the Blue Wahoos logo specifically), and the second image could not be sourced and is assumed non-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: My opinion, and then some other unrelated talk.
item.content:encoded: I was only kinda interested in the SCP (mainly because I've heard the story of someone that doesn't exist existing before), but I got SUPER interested all the sudden when I got to the part where it shows the image with the report of all the viewers eyes were closed. For that part alone I gave it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing hugely wrong here, but nothing that really excited me either. I don't tend to like GOIs, but I tend to just ignore them, so a reference to Oneiroi doesn't bother me. I also know absolutely nothing about baseball, so perhaps that's why I didn't much like it. I don't think it should be removed, so I shall no-vote.

However, I absolutely love your referencing system and the Foundation journals. It makes your articles so much realer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This felt kind of boring untill the last addendum. That was delightfully wierd. However I'm not really sure about Oneiroi's inclusion, that felt a bit tacked on.

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a lifelong Reds fan, I applaud their inclusion in this article. I also love that the Foundation owns a minor league baseball team. It implies that they've had to deal with anomalous baseball players before

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the Oneiroi collective much (it hasn't added anything to any of the SCPs I've seen it in), but this managed to be delightful nonetheless. Very good pacing and escalation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it, but that might mostly be from my love for baseball. This isn't the best SCP I've ever read, but it is certainly a good one. +1

And Tony Cingrani would like a word with this man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly are you getting that the Oneiros are benevolent? Considering that some of their Skips have a rather horrible propensity for turning people's heads into telephones or making nightmares into reality if used wrong, I don't really get any *benevolence* from them. At the very least, the horrible, fey kind of benevolence, where they attempt to be nice without terribly caring about whether the mortals they try to help survive it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciate how most of your SCPs aren't horrifying, they're not world-ending, they're just weird. And containing them necessitates some pretty low-cost, but weird solutions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reluctantly downvoted due to being associated with Oneiroi.

The article itself is passable (I like the part that re-uses a concept from 592), but I'm sorry, but I've never liked the concept of the GOI, and the entry on the GOI page that just got put up makes me able to articulate why. They sound like a Mary Sue GOI:

  • It's on good terms with everyone but the Factory.
  • It's completely mysterious to the Foundation, even more so than Nobody.
  • It can contact "isolated entities" such as Nobody. Not even the Serpent's Hand has been able to do that, to my knowledge.
  • From the look of all the SCPs for them I've read, they are unerringly benevolent.

I made complaints similar to this about the Serpent's Hand, but quite frankly, Oneiroi makes it look like an angsty, flawed JRPG character by comparison. Furthermore, a Group of Interest that's only present in dreams… that is the dumbest concept for a GoI I've ever heard that's not a joke. I'm sorry, but it is.

I realize it's early days yet, and there hasn't been much chance to flesh out Oneiroi's personality, but honestly, that's not much of an excuse. The Manna Charitable Foundation, the last GoI established before this one (Not counting Chicago Spirit, Black Queen or the SPC), had a clearly defined personality by the time it was made a GoI: a well-meaning charity organization that kept screwing up, but in the end, only meant good for the world. Before that, AWCY? had a personality of pretentious hacks that made art because it was "cool" and for no other reason. Both of them were at least partially realized by the time they were put on the GoI list. Oneiroi is just a bunch of cryptic, dream-related SCPs connected by a single word. An arc word does not a group of interest make.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, this is a weird coincidence. The "Homecoming" episode of Welcome to Night Vale (released 15 October 2014) has a major plot point in a football player basically being this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heads up: Link to 1948 leads to 1498. Other than that minor typo, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least 96% of all users and 100% of staff on this wiki are Roget's socks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was reading the comments just to find out of that was a mistake, or if it was the discepancy of 'one of them isn't real' XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a typo in your first addendum. In the subject description, it says "two (1)". I'd du. It myself, but I'm on mobile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, the FT software output is one of the two or three most frightening motifs on the site. Cribbing it is a dangerous game; I think it is hard to use to sufficient effect. It has been a while since I got a chill while reading from recognizing a motif, and a lesser writer might have botched it. +1 for not screwing that up for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was kind of not feeling this because it just seemed like "baseball baseball baseball", but the final addendum did a lot to endear it to me. I will say that the description of the first image is pretty long and convoluted, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Double-blind analysis of aerial sonar readings

If you just mean that the people doing the analysis weren't told anything about what they were analyzing, that would be single blinding, in which case the adjective be "blinded" rather than "double-blind". Of course, that would lead to the problem that readers might think that the analyzers were blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Phenomena" is plural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not the concept that I object to, and I agree the in-universe justification is sensible (hell, I used a similar procedure in one of my own articles) but I think that the addendum presenting the information in exactly the same way doesn't work for me. If the image analysis system was referred to in the article but the logs weren't replicated, I would agree with you.

The presentation was specifically created by SCP-592's author in an impactful way, and I don't like its re-use here because I think that, unlike SCP-592, there's no actual interesting content in that addendum and all the interest comes from a device that has been obviously taken from another popular article. I'd compare it to someone shoe-horning one of the 'yournamehere' codes into an article because they saw SCP-902 do it.

I don't deny that it's possible for someone to use the photoanalysis program in another SCP in a way that I'd upvote, but this SCP, in my opinion, is not it. If you removed that addendum completely and added in a line somewhere else mentioning the eyes-closed thing (which I find odd not to have been mentioned anywhere else), then the only thing lost would have been the format of the log, which is a source of interest that is, undeniably, cribbed from another article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image analysis is pretty much cribbed from SCP-592,

Once again. How is the Foundation using consistent procedure, doubly so one with a solid grounding in real world, ever a bad thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are good things about this, but I'm going to downvote.

I really like the failed containment procedures - they're the most refreshing and interesting procedures I've seen in a long time, and the best I've seen from an author who's pretty good at making off-the-wall procedures. I like the fate of the unfortunate Mr. Potsdam. I like the peripheral footnotes, and I like the images. I don't really understand how the Foundation is supposed to have found out about this, but I suppose there's actually tangible evidence that something odd is going on here, even if it doesn't seem sensible for the Foundation to bust out the sonar when faced with a possible typo.

But I have to agree with Kalinin - there's just not that much here when you get down to it. The idea of a nonexistent baseball player is fine, I guess, but you shoot yourself in the foot by telling us pretty much everything there is to know about the meat of the article in the caption to the first image. I mean, "Ellis Canastota, who does not exist" is a sufficient summary of this article except the aforementioned containment stuff. The image analysis is pretty much cribbed from SCP-592, the backstory is cribbed from Oneiroi, and neither of them really add anything. I was thinking there was going to be some kind of progression here, but it didn't really happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because he apparently didn't exist prior to joining the team, in records or otherwise. I would certainly hope the foundation would notice something like a spontaneously appearing minor league baseball player.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used the wording from the system in 592 after I realized my idea for analysis of infohazardous images was used well in it. Why would the foundation reinvent the wheel when they already have software that does the same thing developed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I missed something, but how did they even find out about this? As far as anyone could tell, it was just an ordinary baseball player, and apparently it took some advanced methods to find any indication otherwise. So why did they even start looking at this 'guy' in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've also gone a couple of bridges too far in terms of borrowing elements from other articles. I found the pretexts for the appropriation of the SCP-592 photoanalysis conceit to be flimsy, to the point that it comes across as a form of coattail-riding.

Dunno. I don't recall ever reading 592, and looking at it, it doesn't even have Kate's vote on it.
I'm tempted to call this convergent evolution.
Besides, even if it was outright taken, I'd still like it because it makes the Foundation appear to have consistent methodics in these cases, and ones that are rather RL-based.

You do have a point in farming out the motive in a way that doesn't even hint at some goal. If this was AWCY? people would be downvoting it in droves.

Either way, call it low standards but I still like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unlike Smapti, I am a baseball aficionado, but baseball is really kind of secondary to the skip.

I had commented on a previous installment of this series that my initial inclination in registering a since-reconsidered downvote was that the peripherals appeared to be obscuring what appeared at the time to be a weak story. This one, unlike that one, is definitely on the wrong end of the peripherals-to-story balance.

Firstly, the story doesn't even really make sense. When this phenomenon was considered to be an actual, living baseball player, why was the Foundation even involved? As far as I can tell, before they found out he didn't exist, to the Foundation this was just another AA-level prospect working on his pitch selection. So why the initial attempts at containment that involved orchestrating a trade to the Foundation Fightin' 682s?

You've also gone a couple of bridges too far in terms of borrowing elements from other articles. I found the pretexts for the appropriation of the SCP-592 photoanalysis conceit to be flimsy, to the point that it comes across as a form of coattail-riding. And the Oneiroi reference seems to be an attempt to farm out, wholesale, any sort of reasoning behind this object, without any real work in the article itself to make that connection.

I'm disappointed in this article. There's not really anything here except borrowed elements from previous articles and a couple of items of mild visual interest. Considered against some of the other pieces of writing in which this is supposed to be contextualized, the comparative lack of narrative quality is glaring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay, your first Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat as usual.
I love the FT analysis of the images as a safe way to deal with infohazards while having the information on the table in its original form. The fact that the entire audience had their eyes closed is just delightfully creepy.

I wonder though, what would happen if the Foundation sent an autonomous robot to interact with the players and/or audience through one of those matches, the bot recording what happens for later viewing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get the impression that I might enjoy this more if I were an afficianado of Minor League Baseball.

Since I'm not an afficianado of Minor League Baseball, or baseball in general, I'm just not feeling it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You and Roget are basically the same person, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Djoric's Oneiroi Initiative

Wot? I got nothing to do with Oneiroi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Baseball is ninety percent mental, and the other half is physical.
- Yogi Berra

New SCP involving Djoric's Roget's Oneiroi Initiative. For more works in this continuity, see Dixieland Nightmare Magic.
